topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The smell of stale coffee and old paper suddenly felt suffocating. I was halfway through cataloging my week in my journal, a private space where I let the anxieties of college life spill out onto the pages. When I returned to my room, the atmosphere had shifted. My desk drawer, usually a fortress of privacy, was slightly ajar. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I noticed the spine of my journal was slightly bent, and a few pages seemed… different. I picked it up, my fingers trembling slightly as I flipped through the entries. A passage I had only written yesterday, a raw outpouring of fear about an upcoming presentation, was underlined in a heavy, dark ink that wasn't mine. My breath hitched. I looked towards the door, half-expecting to see my roommate, Alex, but the room was empty. A wave of sharp, unwelcome panic washed over me, making my hands shake so badly I had to set the book down on the floor.

[story 2]

It started subtly. A misplaced bookmark. Then, the realization hit me like a physical blow when I found my meticulous daily log, the one I kept locked in my binder, open on my bedside table. The handwriting was undeniably mine, but interspersed between my neat script were annotations—Alex’s looping, slightly careless handwriting. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat in the quiet apartment. I stood frozen by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. I took a slow, shallow breath, trying to anchor myself, but every muscle in my body felt tight, coiled. When Alex finally walked in, whistling tunelessly, I didn't look up. I just stared at the floor, my gaze fixed on the scuff marks near the door, hoping the silence would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The silence in the room was too loud. I was trying to focus on my calculus homework, but my attention kept snagging on the side of my desk. My journal, usually tucked neatly under my textbook, was sitting open. I didn't remember leaving it there. My eyes darted around the room, searching for an explanation, a misplaced item, anything to rationalize the unsettling sight. When I finally forced myself to look at the pages, the words were there—my thoughts, my insecurities laid bare. But they were framed by Alex’s commentary, a silent, invasive reading. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I slammed the textbook shut, the sound echoing sharply in the small space. I couldn't move; my legs felt rooted to the floor, and I just stood there, my chest tight, unable to articulate a single word.
